question: What thermodynamic cycle does the NuScale plant use for power conversion?

nuscale_ch01_0439.title: SRP 10.4.6, Rev 3: Condensate Cleanup SystemII.2 Maintain indirect cycle PWR water quality to avoid corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)
nuscale_ch01_0439.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Conformance with Regulatory Criteria
Tier 2 1.9-135 Revision 5
SRP 10.3.6, Rev 3: Steam and 
Feedwater System MaterialsII.2 Fracture Toughness of Class 2 and 3 
ComponentsNot Applicable The NuScale design contains no Class 2 or 3 
components.Not Applicable

CondensersII.1 Prevent excessive releases of 
radioactivity to the environment 
(GDC 60)Conforms None. 10.4.1

Bypass SystemII.2 Residual Heat Removal (GDC 34) Departure T he NuScale design supports an exemption 
from the power provisions of GDC 34. As 
described in Section 3.1.4, the design complies with a NuScale-specific principal 
design criterion in lieu of this GDC.10.4.4

Bypass SystemII.3 MSIV Alternate Leakage Path Not Applicable BWR only. Not Applicable

Water SystemII.1 Flooding of SSC important to safety 
(GDC 4)Conforms None. 10.4.5

Cleanup SystemII.1 Maintain direct cycle BWR plant water 
quality to avoid corrosion-induced 
failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)Not Applicable
BWR only. Not Applicable

Cleanup SystemII.2 Maintain indirect cycle PWR water 
quality to avoid corrosion-induced 
failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (GDC 14)Conforms In the NuScale SG design, the primary water 
is outside the steam generator tubes, the 
secondary water is inside the tubes, and there is no SG blowdown so the secondary 
chemistry requirements for the NuScale 
design differ from those outlined in the referenced EPRI report.10.4.6

Condensate and Feedwater

nuscale_ch01_0439.summary: The section outlines NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for maintaining indirect cycle PWR water quality to prevent corrosion-induced failure of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, as required by GDC 14. It states that the NuScale steam generator design differs from traditional designs, with primary water outside and secondary water inside the tubes, and no steam generator blowdown, resulting in unique secondary chemistry requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0438.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with various regulatory criteria for systems related to condensate cleanup and associated components. It notes that certain requirements are not applicable to NuScale’s design, particularly those specific to BWRs, while confirming compliance with relevant PWR water quality standards to prevent corrosion-induced failures, with specific differences in secondary chemistry requirements due to the unique NuScale steam generator design.

nuscale_ch01_0030.title: Fuel Handling and Storage Systems
nuscale_ch01_0030.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report General Plant Description
Tier 2 1.2-10 Revision 51.2.1.4 Power Conversion System
The power conversion systems associated with  an NPM consist of a main steam system, 
a turbine generator set, a st andard condenser and coolin g tower arrangement, and a 
condensate and feedwater system as shown in Figure 1.2-3. 
With multiple NPMs per plant, individu al NPMs can be placed into service 
incrementally to meet construction schedules and grid demand as permitted by the 
site license. NPMs can also be taken off-line individually for refueling outages and 
maintenance.
1.2.1.5 Fuel Handling and Storage Systems
The fuel handling and reactor maintenance ar eas are located in the west end of the RXB 
and include space for the SFP, refueling pool, and dry dock. The pools are shown in 
Figure 1.2-16.
1.2.1.6 Plant Cooling Water Systems
The plant cooling water systems include several systems that are important to 
supporting plant operation. These systems include the following:
• The reactor component cooling water syst em (RCCWS) is a no nsafety-related, 
closed-loop cooling system that transfers heat from various plant components to the site cooling water system. The RCCW S provides cooling to the CRDMs, the 
non-regenerative heat ex changers for each CVCS, and the primary sampling 
system coolers. (Section 9.2.2)
• The reactor pool cooling system and the spent fuel pool cooling system are 
nonsafety-related, closed-loop systems that transfer heat from the associated pool 
to the site cooling water system. (Section 9.1.3)
• The circulating water system is an open-lo op system that provides a continuous 
supply of cooling water to the plant t urbine condensers. Circulating water pumps 
draw water from a common basin to provide cooling water flow for up to six condensers in one TGB. Heated circulating water from the outlet  of the condensers 
flows to a set of mechanical-draft cooling towers where excess heat is removed as 
the water gravity flows back to the common basin. (Section 10.4.5)
• The site cooling water system is an open-loop system that provides a continuous 
supply of cooling water to the chille d water system, the balance of plant 
component cooling water system, the spent fuel pool cooling system, the reactor 
pool cooling system, the RCCWS, and th e condenser air remo val system. Site 
cooling water pumps draw water from a common basin to provide cooling water 
flow to the systems serviced. Heated site  cooling water from the outlet of the 
individual system heat exchangers continues to a dedicated set of 
mechanical-draft cool ing towers where excess heat is removed as the water gravity 
flows back to the common  basin. (Section 9.2.7)
nuscale_ch01_0030.summary: The fuel handling and storage systems are located in the west end of the Reactor Building (RXB) and include designated areas for the spent fuel pool, refueling pool, and dry dock. These facilities support safe storage and handling of nuclear fuel during refueling and maintenance operations.

references.caption: Schematic of a Single NuScale Power Module and Associated Secondary Equipment

nuscale_ch01_0029.title: Power Conversion System
nuscale_ch01_0029.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report General Plant Description
Tier 2 1.2-10 Revision 51.2.1.4 Power Conversion System
The power conversion systems associated with  an NPM consist of a main steam system, 
a turbine generator set, a st andard condenser and coolin g tower arrangement, and a 
condensate and feedwater system as shown in Figure 1.2-3. 
With multiple NPMs per plant, individu al NPMs can be placed into service 
incrementally to meet construction schedules and grid demand as permitted by the 
site license. NPMs can also be taken off-line individually for refueling outages and 
maintenance.
1.2.1.5 Fuel Handling and Storage Systems
The fuel handling and reactor maintenance ar eas are located in the west end of the RXB 
and include space for the SFP, refueling pool, and dry dock. The pools are shown in 
Figure 1.2-16.
1.2.1.6 Plant Cooling Water Systems
The plant cooling water systems include several systems that are important to

• The reactor component cooling water syst em (RCCWS) is a no nsafety-related, 
closed-loop cooling system that transfers heat from various plant components to the site cooling water system. The RCCW S provides cooling to the CRDMs, the 
non-regenerative heat ex changers for each CVCS, and the primary sampling

• The reactor pool cooling system and the spent fuel pool cooling system are 
nonsafety-related, closed-loop systems that transfer heat from the associated pool 
to the site cooling water system. (Section 9.1.3)
• The circulating water system is an open-lo op system that provides a continuous 
supply of cooling water to the plant t urbine condensers. Circulating water pumps 
draw water from a common basin to provide cooling water flow for up to six condensers in one TGB. Heated circulating water from the outlet  of the condensers 
flows to a set of mechanical-draft cooling towers where excess heat is removed as 
the water gravity flows back to the common basin. (Section 10.4.5)
• The site cooling water system is an open-loop system that provides a continuous 
supply of cooling water to the chille d water system, the balance of plant 
component cooling water system, the spent fuel pool cooling system, the reactor

flow to the systems serviced. Heated site  cooling water from the outlet of the

flows back to the common  basin. (Section 9.2.7)
nuscale_ch01_0029.summary: The Power Conversion System in the NuScale plant includes a main steam system, turbine generator, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, allowing for flexible operation and maintenance of individual modules. The plant also features dedicated systems for fuel handling, storage, and various cooling needs, including closed-loop and open-loop systems to support safe and efficient plant operation.

references.caption: Schematic of a Single NuScale Power Module and Associated Secondary Equipment

nuscale_ch01_0446.title: DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater SystemII.7 Flow Accelerated Corrosion
nuscale_ch01_0446.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Conformance with Regulatory Criteria
Tier 2 1.9-136 Revision 5
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater SystemII.2
Fluid Instabilities (GDC 4) Partially Conforms The intent of this acceptance criterion and 
its subtier guidance - to satisfy GDC 4 related to protecting SSC from fluid flow 
instability effects such as water hammer - is 
applicable. However, much of the specific language in the subtier guidance refers to 
reactor plant designs such as large LWRs, 
and is not relevant to the NuScale plant design.10.4.7
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater 
SystemII.3
Sharing of Structures, Systems, and 
Components (GDC 5)Conforms None. 10.4.7
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater 
SystemII.4 Heat Removal Capability (GDC 44) Not Applicable The CFWS is not a syst em used to transfer 
heat to an ultimate heat sink.Not Applicable
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater SystemII.5
Inspection (GDC 45) Not Applicable The CF WS is not a system used to transfer 
heat to an ultimate heat sink.Not Applicable
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater 
SystemII.6 Testing (GDC 46) Not Applicable The CFWS is not a system used to transfer 
heat to an ultimate heat sink.Not Applicable
DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: 
Condensate and Feedwater 
SystemII.7 Flow Accelerated Corrosion Conforms None. 10.4.7
SRP 10.4.8, Rev 3: Steam 
Generator Blowdown SystemAll Various Not Applicable The NuScale steam generator design does 
not use a blowdown system.Not Applicable
SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary 
Feedwater System (PWR)All Various Not Applicable The NuScale design neither requires nor 
uses an auxiliary feedwater system. The 
NuScale decay heat re moval system (DHRS) 
performs some functions similar to an auxiliary feedwater system. However, 
compared to an auxiliary feedwater system, 
the DHRS differs in its design, operation, and relationship to the small break LOCA 
plant response.Not ApplicableTable 1.9-3: Conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard  Review Plan (SRP) and Design Specific Review 
Standard (DSRS) (Continued)

nuscale_ch01_0446.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Condensate and Feedwater System, specifically regarding flow accelerated corrosion, stating that the design fully conforms to requirements in this area. It also notes that certain criteria related to heat removal, inspection, and testing are not applicable to the NuScale design, and that systems such as steam generator blowdown and auxiliary feedwater are not used in this plant.

nuscale_ch01_0441.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for the Condensate and Feedwater System. It notes partial conformance with fluid instability requirements (GDC 4) due to differences from large LWR designs, full conformance or non-applicability for other criteria, and clarifies that certain systems (e.g., blowdown, auxiliary feedwater) are not used or required in the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0187.title: BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.2 Spray Water pH and Water Chemistry Requirements for Fission Product Removal
nuscale_ch01_0187.content:
NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report Conformance with Regulatory Criteria
Tier 2 1.9-104 Revision 5
BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for 
Emergency Coolant Water for Pressurized Water ReactorsB.2 Spray Water pH and Water Chemistry 
Requirements for Fission Product RemovalPartially Conforms
The intent of a portio n of this acceptance 
criterion is applicable but the specific language refers to SSC that are not in the 
NuScale design. Specifically the NuScale 
design does not use a containment spray system or a sump. However, during ECCS 
operation, ECCS water does collect inside 
the NuScale containment vessel for recirculation back to the reactor core, and 
thus the pH guideline contained in this 
acceptance criterion is applicable to the DCA.6.2.2
BTP 6-1, (March 2007): pH for 
Emergency Coolant Water for 
Pressurized Water ReactorsB.3 Hydrogen Generation from 
Aluminum CorrosionConforms This acceptance criter ion is applicable but 
certain language in BTP 6-1, which would 
be applied by Acceptance Criterion B.3, refers to SSC that are not in the NuScale 
design. Specifically, the NuScale design 
does not use a containment spray system or a sump. However, during ECCS operation, 
ECCS water does collect inside the NuScale 
containment vessel for recirculation back to the reactor core, and thus the intent of this 
acceptance criterion is applicable to the 
DCA.6.3.2
BTP 6-2, Rev 3: Minimum 
Containment Pressure Model 
for PWR ECCS PerformanceAll (B.1 thru 
B.3)Various Not Applicable This guidance is applic able only to PWRs for 
which a postulated LOCA results in core 
uncovery. For the NuScale design, a LOCA 
does not result in core uncovery.Not Applicable
BTP 6-3, Rev 3: Determination 
of Bypass Leakage Paths in 
Dual Containment PlantsAll Various Not Applicable These acceptance criteria (B.1 through B.9) 
are applicable only to large LWRs that 
incorporate both a primary and secondary 
containment. The NuScale containment vessel design does not include a secondary 
containment. Not ApplicableTable 1.9-3: Conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard  Review Plan (SRP) and Design Specific Review 
Standard (DSRS) (Continued)
SRP or DSRS Section, Rev: 
TitleAC AC Title/Description Conformance 
StatusComments Section

All | Various | Conforms | None. | 19.3
nuscale_ch01_0187.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to certain regulatory criteria regarding pH and water chemistry for emergency coolant and fission product removal, as it does not use a containment spray system or sump, but still collects and recirculates ECCS water within the containment vessel. Some criteria, such as those related to minimum containment pressure and bypass leakage in dual containment plants, are not applicable to NuScale due to differences in design, such as the absence of a secondary containment and the lack of core uncovery during a LOCA.

nuscale_ch01_0186.summary: This section outlines NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding the minimum pH requirements for emergency coolant water in pressurized water reactors. While certain aspects of the referenced guidance (BTP 6-1) do not directly apply due to differences in NuScale’s design—such as the absence of a containment spray system or sump—the intent of the minimum pH criterion remains applicable because emergency coolant water is still collected and recirculated within the containment vessel. The section confirms that NuScale’s design meets the relevant acceptance criteria, with noted exceptions explained.

nuscale_ch01_0415.summary: The NuScale plant design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators, and therefore, the associated systems and regulatory criteria (such as GDC 2, 4, 5, 17, 44, 45, and 46) are not applicable. The plant does not rely on AC or DC power for the performance of its safety functions, making these SRP sections irrelevant to its design.

edges.description: Section A refers to maintaining water quality in an indirect cycle PWR, while Section B refers to maintaining water quality in a direct cycle BWR, thus contrasting the thermodynamic cycle types relevant to the NuScale plant.

Section B describes the components of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, which Section A references in the context of regulatory requirements for maintaining water quality and preventing radioactivity release, thus Section B provides the specific system details that Section A's regulatory criteria apply to.

Section B describes the components and arrangement of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, which Section A references in the context of regulatory criteria for maintaining water quality and preventing radioactivity releases, thereby connecting the design implementation (B) to the regulatory requirements (A) for the thermodynamic cycle.

Section B describes the components of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, which Section A references in the context of regulatory criteria for systems such as condensate cleanup and main condensers, thereby connecting the plant's design (B) to the regulatory requirements (A) for the thermodynamic cycle.

Section B describes the components and configuration of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, which provides the specific design information needed to answer the analysis question, while Section A lists regulatory criteria and conformance statements that do not directly specify the thermodynamic cycle used.

Section A and Section B both discuss subsystems (condensate, feedwater, and cleanup systems) that are components of the NuScale plant's power conversion cycle, providing regulatory context for their design and operation.

Section A and Section B both discuss systems (condensate, feedwater, and cleanup) that are components of the NuScale plant's power conversion process, but neither directly identifies the thermodynamic cycle used, instead focusing on regulatory conformance and system-specific requirements.

Section B describes the components of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, including the main steam system and condenser, while Section A discusses the condensate and feedwater system, which is one of the subsystems mentioned in Section B's general description.

Section A and Section B both discuss subsystems (condensate, feedwater, and cleanup) that are components of the NuScale plant's power conversion cycle, providing regulatory context for their design and operation.

Section A and Section B both discuss systems (condensate, feedwater, and cleanup) that are integral to the NuScale plant's secondary side, providing information about the plant's use of a Rankine (steam) cycle for power conversion.

Section B describes the components of the NuScale plant's power conversion system, including the condensate and feedwater system, which Section A discusses in terms of regulatory conformance and design considerations.

Section A discusses the absence of a containment spray system and sump in the NuScale design, while Section B lists conformance with SRP sections related to main condensers and condensate cleanup, both indicating that NuScale uses an indirect (Rankine) cycle typical of PWRs for power conversion.

Section A discusses emergency coolant water chemistry requirements for PWRs and notes that certain systems (like containment spray) are not present in the NuScale design, while Section B references regulatory criteria for BWR direct cycle water quality and main condenser systems, indicating that NuScale's design does not align with BWR direct cycle features but rather with PWR systems.
Section A repeats the content of Section B, both discussing the partial conformance of the NuScale design with GDC 4 regarding fluid instabilities in the condensate and feedwater system.

Section A discusses the applicability of pH and water chemistry requirements for fission product removal in the NuScale design, referencing the absence of certain systems described in Section B, which outlines minimum pH requirements for emergency coolant water.

Section A and Section B both reference NuScale's conformance with SRP chapters related to power conversion system components (e.g., turbine generator, condensate cleanup, main condenser), but neither directly specifies the thermodynamic cycle used, instead focusing on regulatory compliance for system safety and environmental protection.

Section A and Section B both reference NuScale's conformance with SRP chapters related to power conversion system components, but neither directly identifies the thermodynamic cycle used, instead focusing on regulatory compliance for specific systems.
